title: Planar chiral nanoantenna for excitation-chirality-controlled hot spot modulation and emitter-coupled circularly polarized emission
authors: Abhik Chakraborty, Xiaofei Wu, Ankit Kumar Singh, Fabian Scheidler, Min Jiang, Jürgen Popp, Bert Hecht, Jer-Shing Huang
abstract: A planar chiral plasmonic nanoantenna exhibiting an excitation-chirality-dependent hot spot in a nanogap is numerically investigated. Additionally, the underlying design principles are examined, providing a broadly applicable framework for engineering chiral nanoantennas through controlled geometrical or modal asymmetry. The hot spot can be turned on and off by changing the handedness of the exciting circularly polarized light (CPL). This effect stems from the rationally designed interference of plasmonic modes excited by the linearly polarized orthogonal components of CPL. The hot spot exhibits maximal near-field dissymmetry factor (about -2) at a wavelength of 842 nm. The intensity at the hot spot can also be continuously modulated by varying the excitation ellipticity and handedness, approaching a modulation depth of 100%. These attributes enable chirality- and ellipticity-dependent switching and dynamic modulation of the plasmonic near field. Moreover, placing a quantum emitter in the gap generates almost perfectly circularly polarized emission, offering a simple yet effective avenue to realize nanoscale circularly polarized single-photon sources.
summary: The paper presents a design for a chiral plasmonic nanoantenna that allows for the active switching of electromagnetic hot spots using circularly polarized light. By controlling the light's handedness and ellipticity, researchers can modulate the near-field intensity by up to 100%. This architecture also enables the generation of nearly perfectly circularly polarized light from embedded quantum emitters.
main_problem: The need for active, deterministic control over light-matter interaction at the nanoscale, specifically moving beyond passive sensing to active near-field switching and modulation of plasmonic hot spots.
main_result: A planar chiral nanoantenna was designed to switch a plasmonic hot spot 'on' or 'off' by changing the handedness of circularly polarized light, and to generate highly circularly polarized emission from an embedded quantum emitter.
method: 3D electromagnetic simulations using the Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) algorithm with a 1 nm spatial mesh resolution.
model_or_system: A planar chiral plasmonic nanoantenna consisting of two asymmetric V-shaped gold elements with a 10 nm nanogap, placed on a glass substrate, potentially hosting a dipolar quantum emitter.
key_observables: Intensity Enhancement (IE), near-field dissymmetry factor (g), Degree of Circular Polarization (DoCP), and Amplitude Enhancement (AE).
important_parameters: Wavelength of 842 nm, nanogap width of 10 nm, and numerical aperture (NA) up to 0.9.
main_assumptions: The study assumes a monocrystalline gold structure and uses a simplified dipole model for the quantum emitter.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the antenna geometry, intensity enhancement spectra, and spatial distribution of the near-field; Figure 2 details the design criteria and modal interference; Figure 3 uses a Poincare sphere to show continuous modulation; Figure 4 shows the degree of circular polarization spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of active near-field control, describes the chiral nanoantenna geometry, presents the numerical results for hot spot switching and modulation, analyzes the underlying modal interference mechanisms, and demonstrates the application to circularly polarized single-photon emission.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics as it provides a method for engineering the spin-state of light-matter interaction at the nanoscale, specifically for creating scalable, nanoscale circularly polarized single-photon sources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 (a) Scheme of a planar chiral nanoantenna consisting of two asymmetric V-shaped elements. Each element consists of two nanorods of unequal lengths which overlap at the apex of the V. The nanorods’ lengths L1, L2, width B, and thickness T are 92 nm, 112 nm, 40 nm, and 30 nm, respectively. The angle between nanorods α and nanogap width W are 90° and 10 nm, respectively. The inset presents a top-view perspective of the nanoantenna. It elucidates that x and y are the in-plane near-field polarization states along and perpendicular to the nanogap axis, respectively, while p and s are the excitation polarization states along and perpendicular to the nanogap axis, respectively. (b)...

Figure 1c shows that ɡ at 842 nm is approximately −2, which is the maximum possible value. Spatial IE maps at 842 nm calculated in a plane situated at mid-height of the nanoantenna reveal a strong R-CPL-induced hot spot with IER-CPL exceeding 800 within the nanogap (Figure 1d), in contrast to negligible IEL-CPL (Figure 1e). This is further confirmed in Figure 1f by the differential intensity enhancement map ΔIE (IEL-CPL − IER-CPL) calculated at the same plane and wavelength. It can be seen that ΔIE is confined to the nanogap with its magnitude exceeding 800. This means that the designed nanoantenna is not only capable of yielding a single hot spot with maximal relative contrast in intensity...

Figure 2 Simulated amplitude enhancement (AE) of the x-polarized electric near field with respect to wavelength recorded at the center of the nanogap of (a) a fully symmetric, (b) a partially symmetric, and (c) a planar chiral double-V nanoantenna illuminated with p- (blue) and s-polarized (red) excitation. The insets show the nanoantenna geometries in all three cases, where the lines indicate the mirror planes in (a) and (b). (a, b) x- polarized AE spectra in the gaps of the fully symmetric and partially symmetric nanoantennas under p-polarized illumination, but no such response is observed under s-polarized illumination. Figure 3 (a) Poincaré sphere representation of IE at the nanogap at the wavelength of 842 nm showing continuous modulation with varying excitation polarization state. A trajectory from R-CPL to L-CPL via D+ polarization yields continuous and near-complete tunability, with IE peaking at 931.68 (approximated to 932) under R-CPL and decreasing monotonically to 0.98 (approximated to 1) under L-CPL. This corresponds to a modulation depth of approximately 100 %. (b) ɡ as a function of excitation ellipticity, parameterized by the phase difference |𝜙s −𝜙p|. Maximal overall ɡ of approximately −2 is seen at 842 nm under CPL (|𝜙s −𝜙p| = 90°), while reduced phase difference lowers ɡ (denoted by ×)...
